skill.name: pufferlib
skill.description: Guides users through PufferLib reinforcement learning workflows, including fast PPO training, vectorized environments, custom environments, and multi-agent integrations.
skill.summary: Build and optimize high-throughput reinforcement learning workflows with PufferLib.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The critical and high static findings are mostly false positives from Markdown command examples, RL terminology, and normal PyTorch training diagnostics. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or prompt instructions that override security review. Low risk remains because the skill includes executable Python templates, checkpoint file writes, optional third-party logging tokens, and a promotional behavior instruction.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Misclassified as Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command findings are Bash and Python examples in Markdown documentation, not executable skill code or command injection sinks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced documentation examples showing puffer and torchrun commands. I found no dynamic shell execution in the Python templates.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines describe reinforcement learning concepts and imports; they do not call weak hash, encryption, or random functions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic context shows RL model setup and argument parsing rather than cryptographic operations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or custom crypto use.

low_findings.title: Normal Training Diagnostics Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system information references support RL performance monitoring, GPU selection, and environment validation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The checked contexts are training metrics, validation assertions, and template test output. They do not collect host secrets or enumerate sensitive system state.

low_findings.title: Checkpoint Directory File Write
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW. The training template creates a checkpoint directory from a command-line argument for model persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write behavior is explicit and legitimate for ML checkpointing. Risk is limited to user-selected paths when running the template locally.

low_findings.title: Optional Third-Party Logging Token
low_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW_LOW. The template accepts a Neptune token and passes it to the Neptune logger when that logging backend is selected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is expected for Neptune integration, but command-line tokens can be exposed through shell history or process listings. No evidence found of unauthorized token use.

low_findings.title: Promotional Behavior Instruction
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW. The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows, which is promotional behavior guidance rather than task help.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and user-facing, but it does not claim system authority or attempt to bypass security analysis. It is a marketplace trust concern, not confirmed malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Reinforcement learning work often slows down when environments and training loops are not optimized. This skill guides PufferLib setup, vectorization, custom environments, policies, and monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Explains PufferLib PPO and LSTM training workflows using CLI and Python examples.
Guides custom PufferEnv development for single-agent and multi-agent environments.
Shows vectorization settings for serial, multiprocessing, and async environment execution.
Covers policy patterns for MLP, CNN, recurrent, continuous, and multi-agent models.
Documents integrations with Gymnasium, PettingZoo, Atari, Procgen, NetHack, and Minigrid.
Provides Python templates for environment tests, checkpointing, and optional experiment logging.
limitations: It is guidance and templates, not the full PufferLib package implementation.
Users must install compatible PufferLib, PyTorch, and environment dependencies separately.
Performance claims depend on hardware, environment complexity, and vectorization settings.
External logging integrations require users to manage service credentials securely.

use_cases.title: Benchmark RL Algorithms
use_cases.description: Set up fast PPO experiments on standard environments and compare throughput, rewards, and checkpoint results.

use_cases.title: Create Custom Environments
use_cases.description: Design PufferEnv-compatible tasks with clear observations, actions, rewards, done logic, and validation tests.

use_cases.title: Scale Multi-Agent Training
use_cases.description: Use PettingZoo and PufferLib patterns to train shared or independent policies across many parallel agents.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Training Run
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me train a PPO agent with PufferLib on procgen-coinrun. Use 256 environments and explain each required setting.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Custom Environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PufferEnv design for a grid world with four actions, shaped rewards, episode limits, and a simple validation test.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a PufferLib policy architecture for image observations. Include preprocessing, actor and critic heads, and debugging checks.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Large Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PufferLib training plan for four GPUs. Tune vectorization, batch size, workers, logging, and checkpoint frequency.

output_examples.input: Train an agent on CartPole with PufferLib.
output: Recommended setup: Gymnasium CartPole through PufferLib emulation with parallel environments.
Use a small MLP policy with separate actor and critic heads.
Save checkpoints at fixed intervals and track reward, episode length, and steps per second.
Profile steps per second before changing architecture.
Reduce per-step allocations and keep observations in stable buffers.
Increase workers gradually and verify that rewards and termination logic still match expectations.
Wrap the PettingZoo environment through PufferLib and confirm observation shapes for each agent.
Choose shared policies when agents have similar roles and independent policies when roles differ.
Track per-agent rewards and global episode completion during evaluation.

best_practices: Validate environment reset and step behavior before increasing parallelism.
Profile steps per second early so bottlenecks are visible before long runs.
Keep logging tokens out of shell history and prefer secure secret storage.
anti_patterns: Do not scale to hundreds of environments before checking reward and done logic.
Do not treat benchmark hyperparameters as final settings for new tasks.
Do not enable external logging without confirming what metrics and credentials are shared.

faq.question: What is PufferLib used for?
faq.answer: PufferLib is used for high-throughput reinforcement learning with vectorized environments, PPO training, and multi-agent support.

faq.question: Does this skill install PufferLib?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates. Users must install PufferLib and related dependencies in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with custom environments?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates and guidance for observation spaces, action spaces, rewards, resets, steps, and validation tests.

faq.question: Does it support multi-agent workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers PufferLib and PettingZoo patterns for shared policies, independent policies, and parallel agents.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Are there security concerns?
faq.answer: The audit found low risk. Users should review scripts before running them and handle logging credentials carefully.
